data.title: Mother arrested after boy, 12, told police she beat him with a rattan cane
data.description:
Police have arrested a woman on suspicion of child abuse after she allegedly hit her 12-year-old son with a rattan cane in a Hong Kong flat.
The police force said it received a report at 2.48pm on Saturday from a boy in Wong Tai Sin who complained he had been beaten by his mother.
The boy, whose shoulder was red and swollen, was later taken to Queen Elizabeth Hospital in Yau Ma Tei for treatment after police arrived at the public housing flat on Tsui Fung Street.
Officers conducted an initial investigation and arrested the 41-year-old mother on suspicion of “ill-treatment or neglect by those in charge of a child or young person”.
She was detained while the Wong Tai Sin district crime squad conducted further investigations.
The report follows several cases of alleged child abuse or neglect in recent weeks, including a couple arrested earlier this month after their one-year-old son fell to his death from a high-rise residential building in Tsuen Wan.
Police also arrested a 19-year-old man on suspicion of ill‑treating a baby after a video posted online showed him on an MTR train repeatedly slapping the infant’s foot each time it touched the stroller’s front bar.
A source said the teenager was the baby’s maternal uncle.
The government has also strengthened child protection by passing a new law that took effect in January this year.
Under the Mandatory Reporting of Child Abuse Ordinance, more than 100,000 people across 25 professions in the social welfare, education and medical sectors are required to report suspected maltreatment of those aged under 18 or face penalties of up to three months’ imprisonment and a fine of HK$50,000 (US$6,410).

data.title: Plan for full taxi access to Discovery Bay sparks anger among residents
data.description:
Some Discovery Bay residents have expressed frustration over a proposal to allow unrestricted taxi access to the seaside resort-like neighbourhood in Hong Kong, with a petition calling for greater public consultation garnering more than 1,100 signatures.
In a reply to the South China Morning Post on Saturday, the Transport Department also confirmed it had received a taxi proposal from Discovery Bay’s management company, as well as “different views” from residents.
Taxis have had limited access to Discovery Bay since 2014, but it has been restricted to the north plaza of the sprawling residential area developed by HKR International on Lantau Island.
A developer’s notice, dated January 2026 and seen by the SCMP on Saturday, outlined a proposal to add four new taxi ranks in the neighbourhood, including at the Discovery Bay Golf Club, and to allow point-to-point drop-off and pickup.
The notice described existing transport arrangements as “extremely inconvenient” given the community’s development and ageing population, posing a “significant obstacle” to the elderly, those with disabilities and people needing to travel after midnight.
The developer said it had collected 4,000 signatures from residents in Discovery Bay and nearby Peng Chau and Nim Shuen Wan urging the government to address the transport shortage and that it had actively engaged with authorities to explore expanding services.
The developer said it commissioned British professional services firm Arup to conduct a traffic impact assessment and come up with improvement measures.
It added that management would deploy additional security personnel and use artificial intelligence to identify and crack down on any traffic violations.
The full plan was expected to be implemented by the first quarter this year, upon completion of the consultation, the notice said.
But the proposal has sparked concern among some residents, including those who said they were never consulted about the matter.
A petition shared online had garnered 1,186 signatures as of 6pm on Saturday. It described the proposal as a “significant and potentially irreversible change to a community deliberately designed to be low‑traffic, pedestrian‑first and family-friendly”.
It said the consultation process was “inadequate and poorly communicated”, arguing that many residents were not even aware such a process was under way.
The petition said there had been no direct communication with households, tenants or owners, and no public meeting had been held where residents could express their views.
It added that allowing unrestricted taxi access posed safety risks to the community, given the narrow lanes and lack of pavements in many areas.

The petition called for improved public transport coverage and priority door-to-door services for the elderly and those with mobility problems rather than allowing taxis unrestricted access to Discovery Bay.
“We therefore call for an immediate pause to the current process to allow for a proper, transparent and widely communicated consultation period,” the petition said.
The Transport Department said it received a proposal from the management company to allow urban and Lantau taxis to enter Discovery Bay to “meet the needs of the local residents”.
It noted that the management company was conducting local consultations and examining traffic improvement measures to address residents’ concerns.
“The [department] has also received different views from some local residents. We will keep in view the situation,” it said.
Some social media users have vented their frustrations over the lack of consultation.
“Consultation? Are you kidding? Traffic signs for the taxi stands have all been erected already, though they are still covered,” one user said.
Another user noted: “The roads within Discovery Bay are private roads, and their maintenance costs are covered by residents’ management fees.
“The large number of profit-making taxis [using] these private roads while residents foot the bill is unfair to the residents.”
But not everyone was entirely opposed to allowing greater taxi access to the mainly car-free neighbourhood that is home to about 20,000 people.
One user said many residents were becoming older and faced difficulties getting home from the single taxi drop-off point, and might require multiple forms of transport even if they were wheelchair users.
“Discovery Bay is for everyone,” the user said.
The SCMP has contacted HKR International for comment.
This is not the first controversy over expanding taxi services in Discovery Bay.
Back in 2021, a similar proposal by a district councillor for Peng Chau, a neighbouring outlying island, also faced pushback from residents and complaints about a lack of consultation.
Peng Chau residents could take a ferry to Discovery Bay to transfer to public transport but the bus stop was some distance from the ferry terminal, the councillor noted.

data.title: Hong Kong police arrest 5, recover nearly HK$100 million in stolen gold bars
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested five people and recovered nearly HK$100 million (US$12.7 million) in gold stolen in a suspected deception case, in which a buyer allegedly worked as an accomplice of a group that seized the haul before fleeing.
The incident took place at about 2.45pm on Friday at an office in Peninsula Square on Sung On Street in Hung Hom, where a staff member was conducting a gold handover to an alleged buyer.
The South China Morning Post had earlier reported that the case was being treated as “obtaining property by deception” and that three individuals were believed to have fled the scene.
Police also said a white vehicle intercepted near a toll plaza on North Lantau Highway during initial inquiries was later confirmed to be unrelated.
Chief Inspector Alex Yau Yu-sing of the West Kowloon regional crime unit said on Saturday that the “buyer” that day was later found to be part of the group and had entered the premises under the guise of carrying out a legitimate transaction.
“Due to the client’s request for privacy, other staff members had temporarily left the unit,” Yau said.
“During the handover, several individuals entered the unit and removed the gold.”
The chief inspector added that they entered the premises claiming the company’s owner owed them a debt.

Together with the “buyer”, they removed dozens of gold bars, packed them into suitcases and left, as a fifth accomplice acted as a lookout outside. The group then fled in two private vehicles.
Staff tried to stop them but were unsuccessful, and the case was later reported to police.
Yau said five suspects were subsequently identified after officers analysed intelligence and reviewed security footage.
Four men and one woman, all Chinese nationals holding Hong Kong identity cards, were arrested later on Friday night in Central and Tai Po.
Police said the case involved 73kg (161lbs) of gold bars worth about HK$93 million, all of which have since been recovered. No injuries were reported.
Yau also said one of the suspects was the mastermind, while others included his associates or employees.
The buyer involved in the transaction and his wife were also among those arrested, and one suspect was found to have had previous business dealings with the company’s owner.
The five suspects remain in custody as investigations continue.
Yau urged the public to avoid disclosing details of high-value transactions and to consider using professional security services when handling valuable goods.

data.title: Flying start for low-altitude economy goals as 100 drone projects proposed
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities have received more than 100 applications for innovative drone usage to pioneer the city’s “low-altitude economy”, with some pilot projects set to launch as early as in three months, the transport minister has said.
Secretary for Transport and Logistics Mable Chan also pledged to push more advanced transport technologies to transition from trials to everyday use in 2026.
In a blog post on the Transport and Logistics Bureau’s website on Saturday, Chan said “the industry responded enthusiastically” to the government’s calls, with more than 100 submissions received by the end of last month, when applications closed. The low-altitude economy is an emerging sector focused on activities such as drone use in airspace below 1km (3,280 feet).
“The [government] working group is reviewing the applications in detail, with the goal of launching pilot projects progressively in the first half of this year,” Chan said.
In line with the nation’s development of a low-altitude economy, Hong Kong leader John Lee Ka-chiu announced in his policy address in 2024 that a government working group would be set up to foster and facilitate the testing of potential application scenarios for low-altitude flying activities.
Promoting “regulatory sandbox” pilot projects was among its major tasks.
The sandbox aims to enable the industry to test and conduct trials on some project concepts along predefined air routes and in a controlled and safe manner. It also allows the government to collect data to ensure applications work and meet safety and reliability requirements.
Based on the success of the first batch of sandbox projects last year, the government launched a new round of applications under a “regulatory sandbox X” scheme late last year. Applications closed in February.
Thirty-eight pilot projects were selected in the first batch, including one using drones to deliver medicine from Cyberport in Pok Fu Lam to St John Hospital on Cheung Chau.
“Together with the around 20 projects from last year’s ‘regulatory sandbox’ that have already entered regular and scaled-up operations, the development of the low-altitude economy will create abundant opportunities for talent across different sectors,” Chan wrote.
She also painted a rosy picture of the low-altitude economy’s development in 2026, describing it as a “pivotal year” in which advanced transport technologies would move from experimental concepts to full-fledged industries.
“We expect multiple innovative transport technologies to transition from testing environments into everyday life this year, reinforcing my conviction that what we are witnessing is not merely a vision of future technological innovation, but the emergence of future industries capable of driving real‑world development,” Chan added.

The minister also said that drone-related professions, from researchers and engineers to operators, would also become “new quality career pathways” for young people.
“Hong Kong is actively planning talent development across multiple fronts to provide fertile ground for their growth,” Chan said.
At present, eight Civil Aviation Department-accredited organisations offer drone operator certification training and assessment. Non-local qualifications can also be recognised upon application.
The Hong Kong Productivity Council, a sandbox partner, is preparing new courses for so-called Category C small drones weighing between 25kg and 150kg, and also long-range beyond-visual-line-of-sight operations.
The Vocational Training Council has already provided training to more than 500 students in areas including drone operation, smart transport and aerial photography.
The Hong Kong International Aviation Academy is also applying to become an approved training organisation to offer courses to train drone pilots and maintenance personnel.
Meanwhile, the University of Hong Kong and Polytechnic University have also launched master’s programmes related to the low‑altitude economy, covering flight principles, airspace management, aircraft design and infrastructure engineering, according to Chan.

data.title: China’s exports are booming – so why are plants in this factory hub closing?
data.description:
As China undergoes a sweeping economic transition, its regions are also in the process of embracing change. The powerhouses of yesteryear must adapt or risk falling behind, as traditional industries become less reliable growth drivers and new sectors take prominence. In this series, we explore three representative areas of the country as they attempt to navigate this rapidly changing environment.
One morning in December, Zhang Li showed up for work as normal at Everwin Toys in Dongguan to find a notice pinned to the factory gates.
The message was brief and unsentimental: due to shrinking demand in the toy industry and changes in the global economy, the company had seen its revenues decline by over 40 per cent and had no choice but to halt production.
For Zhang, the news threw her life into disarray. Like many of her 2,000 colleagues, she had worked at the factory for more than 15 years, joining in her early 20s and spending her prime years on the assembly line.
“Now I’m in my 40s,” she said. “Where will I find my next job?”
Zhang is one of millions of migrant workers caught on the front line of a rapid – and, at times, painful – economic transition unfolding in China, as the country’s vast manufacturing sector adapts to rising global trade tensions and breakneck technological changes.
The shifts are leaving China’s factory hubs feeling increasingly polarised: while the top firms surge ahead – helping China rack up a record trade surplus of over US$1 trillion last year – the reality for workers often feels very different as they face stagnant wages and waves of factory closures.
This duality is particularly acute in Dongguan – a city of 10.5 million in south China’s Guangdong province – that has been central to the country’s export boom since the 1980s, earning it the nickname the “world’s factory”.
In one town alone, there are over 500 hectares of vacant factory space … and Dongguan has 30 towns
Liang Lu, manufacturing firm executive
On paper, Dongguan’s economy is doing well. The city’s gross domestic product grew 4 per cent year on year in 2025, just below the national average. Local exports soared by 9.1 per cent year on year, surpassing 970.7 billion yuan (US$141 billion).
Yet on the ground, another picture emerges. In the city’s industrial zones, factory lots are sitting empty and rents are falling sharply. Migrant workers say their incomes have stagnated – or, in many cases, fallen – over the past five years.
A string of high-profile factory shutdowns hit the city last year, causing thousands of job losses, according to industry insiders. In June, Canadian tech giant Celestica closed its Dongguan facility after 21 years, citing global supply chain pressures. In October, Taiwan’s Kinpo Electronics moved its entire production line to Thailand.
Liang Lu, an executive at a manufacturing firm, was struck by the situation in Dongguan during a recent visit to attend a local investment promotion event.
“In Daojiao town alone, there are over 500 hectares (1,235 acres) of vacant factory space, the local officials mentioned,” Liang said. “And Dongguan has 30 towns, each facing similar vacancy pressures.”
The empty lots testify to the pressure businesses in the “world’s factory” are facing on several fronts.

On one side, there is China’s trade war with the United States, which has squeezed local exporters’ bottom lines – especially in traditional industries like textiles, toys and apparel, where keeping costs low is essential. Many have shifted production to third countries in an attempt to avoid the higher duties.
On the other, there are rapid technological changes affecting producers in a slew of industries, as advances in artificial intelligence, robotics and other emerging sectors threaten to make old business models obsolete.
Dongguan aims to ride out these shifts by moving up the value chain: upgrading existing industries and nurturing emerging ones. Buzzwords like robots, smart terminals and clean energy technology now feature heavily in local policy documents.
Some firms are venturing into cutting-edge fields such as embodied intelligence. Toymakers, meanwhile, are striving to boost their margins by developing their own brands, following the example of Labubu maker Pop Mart.
These efforts have had some success: the city’s latest census showed strong growth in employment in tech-intensive industries such as electronic devices, communications equipment and equipment manufacturing between 2018 and 2023. And local headline growth figures remain impressive.
But there have also been widespread job losses in low-end manufacturing, as local industry becomes increasingly automated and capital-intensive. China had installed more than 2 million industrial robots by the end of 2024, leading large numbers of assembly workers to be replaced.

With demand for labour shrinking and manufacturers facing a volatile trade environment, factory workers in Dongguan are finding it tougher than ever to secure stable jobs at decent wages.
“We are still hiring workers at salaries from several years ago – working extended hours across six-day weeks, with monthly pay of around 5,500 yuan (US$790),” said Zhong Xiao, a recruitment agent based in the city.
Local rents are also plunging. “Compared with two years ago, industrial rents in Dongguan have fallen by about 30 per cent,” said Lawrence Wong, a veteran factory owner in the city. “Vacancy rates have risen sharply … as many facilities have relocated or downsized.”
Wong plans to move with the times: he is now preparing to open the first phase of a new factory in Vietnam, even though commercial real estate there is more expensive than in Dongguan.
This is not the first time Dongguan has stood at a crossroads. As one of China’s first cities to integrate into the global supply chain, it has weathered numerous rounds of economic transformation – and repeatedly defied predictions that it was “over”.
In the 1990s and 2000s, Dongguan’s factories led the “made in China” revolution: churning out everything from shoes to plastic toys for global brands. But overseas orders collapsed after the 2008 financial crash, and the city’s GDP growth slowed dramatically – falling from 18.1 per cent in 2007 to 5.3 per cent in 2009.
The local economy remained under pressure for years afterwards, causing a slew of firms in labour-intensive industries to either fold or flee to cheaper markets.
Dongguan is still important, but it is no longer at the centre
David Wong, Hang Seng University of Hong Kong
With local jobs scarce, the sex trade became rampant: it employed more than 200,000 people at its peak, according to domestic media reports, though no official figures were ever released. Local authorities had to launch a clampdown in 2014 to bring the trade under control.
But Dongguan eventually bounced back. From the mid-2010s, the city emerged as a hub for manufacturing smartphones and automated equipment, as China’s factories raced to integrate new technologies. Then, local firms benefited from the boom in global demand for goods sparked by the Covid-19 pandemic.
This time, however, might be different. Dongguan faces greater headwinds than during past transitions, making it harder for the city to attract capital and talent.
It is no longer one of China’s only gateways to the global market; other cities have emerged as leaders in hi-tech industries; and local government finances are stretched amid China’s property downturn, which has hit Guangdong province particularly hard, according to David Wong, a lecturer at the Hang Seng University of Hong Kong.
“Dongguan is still important, but it is no longer at the centre,” Wong said. “It now functions primarily as an intermediate production hub in the manufacturing chain: indispensable to global manufacturing, but unlikely to become a true innovation engine.”
Even some of Dongguan’s best-known companies are feeling the strain. Topstar Technology – a listed company that makes sensors, industrial robots and other automation equipment – was held up as a success story during Dongguan’s earlier push into smart manufacturing.

Now, the company is exploring more advanced fields such as AI-driven robots, aiming to pioneer the next generation of automation technology. But the company is facing challenges. In 2024, it posted its first annual loss since going public in 2017, after years of volatile earnings.
The firm attributed the loss to ongoing efforts to restructure its business. Public announcements indicate Topstar’s downstream clients are concentrated in the electronic devices, lithium battery and solar photovoltaic industries – sectors currently grappling with intense competition and oversupply problems.
Other firms in Dongguan appear to be facing a similar dilemma: the old model is failing, while the new one has yet to deliver stable returns.
Local business leaders like Steve Huang, who runs a factory making transmission shafts, are just hoping that if they continue to forge ahead, their efforts will finally begin to pay off.
“Over the past few years, I’ve continuously invested in automating production lines and actively cultivated a more diverse overseas client base,” Huang said. “Now, I’m willing to take orders that will deliver a loss of up to 5 per cent, as long as the factory can keep operating and survive until the economy is on the up again.”

data.title: Veteran activist warns of ‘shrinking space’ for green advocacy in Hong Kong
data.description:
After decades at the frontline campaigning for environmental policies, Edwin Lau Che-feng, founder of The Green Earth, is finally slowing down to focus on his health and passion for radio-controlled boats.
The 68-year-old has been one of the most vocal environmentalists championing the waste-charging scheme for more than two decades.
Lau still remembers May 27, 2024, the day when the government abruptly paused the waste-charging bill, as if it were yesterday.
The suspension was the biggest blow in his 36-year career. “It feels impossible. Never in my life have I heard of a law in Hong Kong that has already been passed, yet fails to be implemented,” he said.
The pay-as-you-throw policy, which required residents to use designated prepaid trash bags, was first proposed in 2004 and passed by the legislature in 2021. It was to take effect in 2023 after 18 months of preparation.
But the government shelved the scheme indefinitely after two delays due to widespread public concerns over its impact on the cost of living and uncertainty in the implementation.
“Authorities could have led with waste charging within government premises first,” Lau said. “The government lacked the determination and confidence, as well as the initiative to take action and implement this bill.”
Reflecting on the ups and downs of his career, Lau recalled fond memories of changing sceptics’ minds, and addressed the challenges of the government’s dwindling public engagement, warning of a “borrowed future” in which Hongkongers live at the expense of the next generations.
His journey started in 1989 when, in his early thirties, he came across a newspaper ad and joined Friends of the Earth, rising through the ranks to CEO over 26 years.
In the early 1990s, Lau pioneered the waste paper recycling programme in schools, promoted three-colour recycling bins in public housing estates and pushed for the idling engine ban.
Sustainability was a rare focus then, and Lau often got the cold shoulder from the commercial sector when pitching green measures.
“I developed a cast-iron heart – rejections just slide right off me,” he said with a chuckle. “When you find your mission in life, there is no such thing as overtime [work].”
His proudest achievement came in 2004, when a year-long protest by green groups blocked a decision by developers to demolish all seven residential blocks of the never-occupied, fully refurbished Hunghom Peninsula estate to redevelop luxury flats.
Dubbed the “world’s largest demolition of new buildings”, the move was expected to create 190,000 tonnes of construction waste and substantial pollution.

The plan was withdrawn after the civil society protested for nine months.
“That campaign was an uphill battle against developers, who planned to make billions out of the redevelopment project,” he said.
“But demolishing the estate would send a wrong moral message to the young, telling them that money could override everything, including environmental protection.”
He cited this as an example of how mounting public pressure could bring positive changes in Hong Kong decades ago.
Lau said the trust he built with the government over the years led to his appointment to various advisory bodies, such as committees on environmental campaigns and waste reduction in the 1990s, and the Food Wise Hong Kong Steering Committee and the Advisory Council on the Environment in the 2010s.
In 2009, he was awarded Hong Kong’s Medal of Honour for his “valuable contribution to the promotion of environmental protection and education” by then chief executive Donald Tsang Yam-kuen.
“In the early days, green groups had a key role in defending the government’s push for environmentally friendly policies, and explaining the rationale to the public as a credible third party,” he said, noting a greater sense of openness from the government.
“However, in recent years, it appears the government no longer treats us as an ally but someone getting in their way, and the relationship has been more tense.”
For instance, the government has substantially reduced meetings with green groups and recycling businesses since 2019 and the Covid-19 pandemic, though they are still engaged in major events such as policy address consultation sessions.
“It has been harder to meet with officials. The space for advocacy has been shrinking,” Lau said.
With fewer chances to speak to the government directly, Lau focused on amplifying voices through the media.
“I feel grateful that the media were open to listening and covering green policies from a neutral perspective and made space [for us] to voice out,” he said.
“If this [channel] is gone as well, it will be a huge blow.”

The importance of speaking up is what eventually led Lau to start over in his late fifties.
When Friends of the Earth moved away from policy advocacy in 2015, Lau chose to move on, leaving the organisation after 26 years.
Determined to keep the conversation with the public and government going, he founded The Green Earth only three months later, in February 2016.
In a small office in Kwai Hing, Lau started a three-person team focused on policy advocacy, driven by his faith in the strong impact of policy changes.
While Lau stepped down as The Green Earth’s executive director last August, the advocate said he was happy with what the NGO had achieved as it marked its 10th anniversary this year, despite it being “twice the effort for half the results”.
Focused on data-driven and policy research advocacy, The Green Earth conducted waste audits, promoted sustainable development of hiking trails, in addition to waste reduction lobbying and education efforts.
Lau said Hong Kong must stop treating sustainability as an “add-on” but blend the concept into every policy.
Citing the waste pyramid model, he noted the government was focused more on recycling and waste treatment, which aligned with the bottom of the pyramid – effective in tackling but not preventing waste.
“The most cost-effective strategy is waste avoidance and reduction, using the least resources to achieve the largest effect,” Lau said.
According to WWF data in 2021, Hong Kong’s ecological footprint was ranked third-worst in the Asia-Pacific region and 14th globally.
Commenting on the city’s environmental movement, Lau said: “The resources we use are borrowed from the future time, future people. If we do not have a mindset to treasure existing resources, [they] may be depleted soon.”

data.title: Labubu, Blackpink’s Jennie draw thousands to ComplexCon in Hong Kong
data.description:
Popular doll Labubu and Blackpink’s Jennie were among the headline names and brands from the city and abroad that drew thousands of fans to Hong Kong’s third edition of pop culture festival ComplexCon early on Saturday.
Organisers said this year’s edition broke attendance records and estimated tens of thousands would show up for the two-day event.
On Saturday morning, attendees took selfies with sculptures of the iconic figurine and queued for exclusive merchandise, while others explored local vinyl record social enterprises and T-shirt brands.
At the opening ceremony, Secretary for Culture, Sports and Tourism Rosanna Law Shuk-pui said ComplexCon presented an “impressive and dynamic combination” of pop culture programmes and was backed by the government’s Mega Arts and Cultural Events Fund for a third year.
“I am delighted to see that our city’s distinctive arts and culture DNA makes it the perfect home for this global convergence of style, trainers, music,” Law said.
She added that beyond this year’s headliners Jennie and American rapper Yeat, she was excited that Hong Kong’s own renowned artist Lung Ka-sing, creator of Labubu, was the festival’s artistic director.
“I am confident that this year’s ComplexCon from Hong Kong will not only resonate globally but also feel local,” Law said.
Lung, Hong Kong-born artist raised in the Netherlands, met fans at the venue on Saturday morning.
Diehard Labubu fan Zhang Tianwei bought a VIP ticket and planned his Asia work trip around ComplexCon. He flew to Hong Kong a few days earlier to grab the special merchandise and meet Lung at the event.
The 34-year-old, who works in fashion in London, proudly showed off his Labubu dolls and tags, each freshly signed by Lung with his autograph drawn as whimsical eyebrows on Saturday. He also bought exclusive ComplexCon Labubu T-shirts at around HK$400 (US$51) to HK$700, along with a pouch.

Collecting the figurine has become a hobby for Zhang.
“Deep within Labubu is a whole universe … few things have given me such pure joy these days,” Zhang said.
He added he was drawn to the figurine given its roots in Hong Kong. “It is a source of pride,” he said.
Other Hong Kong brands have also taken part at ComplexCon.
Edward Man Ho-wai, founder of Club Soul Good, a social enterprise under Sham Shui Po-based non-profit ChickenSoup Foundation, brought vinyl records to ComplexCon, featuring music including that of the late Hong Kong superstar Leslie Cheung Kwok-wing.
As a charity partner of the festival, Man said proceedings from vinyl sales would be used to build projects to support families in need and provide music-related vocational training to transform their lives.
“Music is a special thing, it helps people to heal traumas and be connected to society, such as an international event like ComplexCon,” Man said.

Kenji Wong Shu-keung, founder of Hong Kong-based clothing brand GrowthRing & Supply, hoped to introduce his brand to new customers rather than focusing on sales at ComplexCon.
Visitors who checked out exclusive stencil T-shirts at his booth came from the United States, Taiwan and Japan, he said.
“Could Hong Kong brands become as popular to people overseas like Hong Kong films? I think it is possible, though it takes time,” Wong said.
The pop cultural festival was an approved project by the government’s Mega Arts and Cultural Events. It secured HK$15 million in funding each of the past two years.
Organiser Complex China’s CEO, Bonnie Chan Woo Tak-chi, thanked the attendees for a “record-breaking box office” this year, noting “tens of thousands” would show up today.
“The fans are the heartbeat of everything that we do,” Chan said.
She added that she had “tears of joy” when Lung accepted the invitation to lead the event. “The third edition is especially endearing to the city of Hong Kong because of one very special person,” Chan said.
Blackpink’s Jennie is set to perform Sunday.
ComplexCon was first launched in Los Angeles in 2016, bringing together world-famous urban culture and music artists.
Last year’s ComplexCon, which ran for three days, drew 35,000 visitors and generated on-site business turnover of HK$87.5 million, according to the organiser.

data.title: Global drug giants double down on China amid push for self-reliant supply chains
data.description:
AstraZeneca said it would establish drug manufacturing facilities in Guangzhou and Shanghai as well as a separate innovation centre in Shanghai, joining a group of foreign players building new plants in China amid geopolitical tensions.
The moves are part of a US$15 billion commitment the British firm pledged to make in the world’s second-largest drug market through 2030, unveiled during UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing in January.
China’s manufacturing capabilities, integrated supply chain and cost advantages were drawing multinationals to produce locally, said Zhang Jialin, head of China healthcare research at Nomura. “Beyond their long-term commitment to the China market, technical know-how and quality advantages are key considerations driving these companies to build here.”
On Thursday, AstraZeneca signed a memorandum of understanding with Guangzhou authorities to build a radioconjugate drug manufacturing site, which would produce next-generation cancer drugs for patients in China and the broader Asia-Pacific region, according to a statement on its website.
The new facility will be in the Guangzhou Economic and Technological Development Zone, a state-designated industrial district spanning 17.67 sq km in Huangpu district, east of the city centre. It will specialise in producing an actinium-225-based radioconjugate for prostate cancer treatment.

Separately, AstraZeneca said it planned to build a cell therapy production base in Shanghai’s Lingang Special Area, a government-designated free-trade zone designed to attract advanced manufacturing and hi-tech industries, as well as an innovation centre at Zhangjiang Hi-Tech Park, a life sciences and tech hub in the Pudong district that is home to hundreds of pharmaceutical and biotech companies.
The Shanghai hub will manufacture and supply CAR-T cell therapies, a personalised cancer treatment engineered from a patient’s own immune cells, in China and other Asian markets.
The company said the Shanghai facilities would make it the first global biopharmaceutical firm with end-to-end cell therapy capabilities in China, covering early-stage research, viral vector and plasmid development, clinical manufacturing and regulatory support.
Also on Thursday, AstraZeneca joined hands with the Shanghai Municipal Science and Technology Commission, the University of Glasgow, King’s College London and HSBC to launch the Shanghai-UK Life Science Innovation Ecosystem Initiative, aimed at linking Chinese innovation with global scientific and financial resources to accelerate healthcare development.
Radioconjugates, a transformative approach to cancer treatment, offered “more personalised and precise care while potentially improving on the outcomes of traditional radiotherapy”, Iskra Reic, AstraZeneca executive vice-president for international, said in the Chinese statement.
“The Greater Bay Area offers world-class manufacturing infrastructure and a mature biopharmaceutical ecosystem,” Reic added.
The Guangzhou base would work in tandem with a separate cell therapy manufacturing and innovation centre in Shanghai, according to the company.
The announcements come as AstraZeneca faces scrutiny in China. The company has been under investigation since last year over alleged irregularities involving the import of oncology drugs.
AstraZeneca is not alone in deepening its China manufacturing footprint. Eli Lilly this month announced a US$3 billion investment over the next decade to build a localised production and supply chain in China.
The centrepiece of the plan involves expanding its Suzhou facility and building high-volume manufacturing capacity for orforglipron, its first oral GLP-1 obesity drug currently under regulatory review in China.
Swiss drug maker Roche last year pledged 2.04 billion yuan (US$296 million) to build a second biopharmaceutical manufacturing facility at Zhangjiang Hi-Tech Park, which will produce its eye drug Vabysmo and is expected to be completed in 2029.
Pharmaceutical giants were building self-reliant supply chains in Europe, the US, China and Japan, according to Tony Ren, head of Asia healthcare research at Macquarie Capital. “In case there is a pause in shipments between any of these two regions, the local production can meet local demand,” he said.
“US, China and Japan are the top three national pharmaceutical markets in the world. No rational [company] would want to decouple from any of them, despite political pressure,” he added.

data.title: Hong Kong to issue weekly updates on fuel price changes from April 1
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities will issue weekly announcements about the changes in international and local fuel costs from April, following mounting suspicion that businesses are prematurely raising prices because of the United States-Israeli war on Iran.
The announcement on Saturday also came after the conflict’s impact on oil prices prompted Hong Kong’s transport sector to consider temporary surcharges to offset rising costs.
“We are currently collecting and organising data. Starting from April 1, we will release some comparison reports showing the trend of discounted prices from each fuel company in Hong Kong and international refined oil prices,” Secretary for Environment and Ecology Tse Chin-wan told a radio programme.
“We will release this information to the public once a week, allowing residents to monitor oil price changes more accurately.”
Tse said the updates would allow residents to see whether fuel companies were raising prices too fast or acting too slowly to bring them back down again.
Hong Kong’s fuel is mainly supplied by mainland China, according to the minister.
He added that Hong Kong’s supply was stable despite the city not storing fuel as a strategic reserve.
“There will not be an unstable supply in the foreseeable future,” he said. “Of course, pricing needs to be handled through market mechanisms.”
The Environment and Ecology Bureau said in a social media post it had met with major local fuel suppliers, reiterating the vital importance of energy to Hong Kong’s economic and social operations and urging them to maintain a stable supply.
It added that all suppliers had said the current supply of fuel for vehicles in Hong Kong remained normal.

The bureau also touched on public concerns about paying more at the pump, citing suppliers as saying that compared with the sharp increase in prices for refined oil and vehicle fuel in other places, local levels remained relatively restrained.
“Starting April 1, the Environment and Ecology Bureau will release weekly updates to the public on the discounted retail prices of unleaded petrol and diesel from various vehicle fuel suppliers, as well as the market benchmark prices of international refined petroleum products during the same period,” it said.
“This will help the public monitor the trends, relationships and changes between local vehicle fuel retail prices and international refined petroleum product prices.”
Oil markets have been volatile since conflict broke out in the Middle East at the end of February, forcing Persian Gulf producers to cut output amid the effective closure of the strategic Strait of Hormuz, through which 20 per cent of the world’s supply passes.
The price of Brent crude, an international benchmark, climbed past US$110 per barrel on Friday, down from US$118 earlier in the week, which was the highest since 2022, when it rose above US$120. Before the war, it was trading in the low to mid-US$70s.
As of Saturday morning, regular petrol cost HK$31.09 (US$3.97) per litre, while premium was offered at HK$32.89 at all stations except for PetroChina’s, where it was HK$31.09, according to the Consumer Council’s Oil Price Watch.
The net prices after walk-in discounts for regular petrol range from HK$22.89 at Caltex to HK$22.49 at Esso, while premium petrol prices range from HK$20.59 at PetroChina to HK$31.29 at Esso.

data.title: Refugee in Hong Kong could win global award. So why does she have mixed feelings?
data.description:
When Uzma Naveed discovered she was a finalist for a global award in Paris, it should have been the proudest moment of her life. Instead, she met the news with what she described as “mixed feelings”.
The 37-year-old refugee from Pakistan was chosen from a pool of 1,500 nominees from 97 different jurisdictions for the “national impact” category of the 2026 Women Changing the World Awards, an annual event meant to celebrate women’s achievements.
Uzma, who first came to Hong Kong seeking asylum with her family in 2015, has spent recent years speaking about the experience of refugees in the city in a bid to build empathy among the public and help change the typically negative portrayal of her community.
But even as she pored over the details of the award, scheduled for April 21 to 24, a more sober reality set in – even though her case had been substantiated three years ago, her status in Hong Kong means she is not allowed to leave the city.
“I was telling my friends that I couldn’t believe it. Me, a ‘woman changing the world,’ I’ve never thought of myself like that,” she told the South China Morning Post, describing the initial amazement she felt upon finding out she was a finalist.
“And then it just struck me – I cannot go,” she said, “and I wish I could go because this is one of the biggest opportunities for anyone to be seen in this kind of global forum.”
Hong Kong does not grant asylum, as it is not a signatory to the 1951 UN Refugee Convention. Instead, it provides non-refoulement, or the assurance that individuals will not be sent back to their home countries until their claims are determined.
Only about 1 per cent of all claims are successful in Hong Kong, a process which can take years and during which they are prohibited from working.
Even if their claims are substantiated, they still have to wait for resettlement to a third country – an outcome which has become increasingly uncertain as Western nations retool their resettlement policies.
Throughout the whole process, they remain effectively stateless and unable to leave the city.

Jeffrey Andrews, a senior manager at Christian Action’s Centre for Refugees, said he had handled numerous cases of talented refugees, including young people, who had to forgo similar opportunities due to their inability to leave the city.
He said he hoped Uzma’s experience would help more of the city’s residents understand the contributions refugees make to Hong Kong.
“They are adding so much value and contributions for Hong Kong, especially in terms of diversity, capabilities and accomplishments for the city,” he said.
Uzma came to Hong Kong with her husband and firstborn son in 2015. The family was fleeing religious persecution in Pakistan, and had sold everything they owned to pay an agent to help get them out of the country, she said.
After a series of initial rejections and the birth of her second son, her family’s case would eventually be substantiated at the High Court in 2023.
Since then, she has been working as a community outreach coordinator at Christian Action’s refugee centre – a privilege granted to some substantiated claimants by the director of immigration on a case-by-case basis.
As part of her role, she runs community development initiatives as well as a programme for women’s empowerment. She has also been delivering talks across Hong Kong, including at schools, to challenge negative stereotypes about the community.
She said she was motivated to speak out as she felt there was a “taboo” about refugees in Hong Kong.
Uzma said she wanted to change the negative associations commonly held by the public, noting that they were usually portrayed as criminals in the media.
Most refugees were grateful to be safe in Hong Kong and just wanted to be given the opportunity to integrate into the community, she said.
“We are all human beings. Give us a chance. We want to give it back to the community. We want to give back to Hong Kong,” she said.

Last November, she delivered a 13-minute speech at Hong Kong’s TEDxTinHauWomen event – the first refugee woman in Hong Kong to do so – which garnered a standing ovation.
One of the people who saw her speak that day nominated her for the Women Changing the World Awards.
Uzma said that some people have encouraged her to ask the Immigration Department for some form of permission to leave Hong Kong for the awards.
But she has decided against pursuing that, saying she does not want to deal with what she sees as an inevitable rejection.
Instead, she said that, for the time being, she is just grateful to have been recognised and for helping to bring wider understanding about the refugee community in Hong Kong.
“Being seen is one of the biggest achievements for me,” she said.
Asked by the SCMP whether it would consider allowing substantiated claimants to apply for a form of travel pass in exceptional circumstances, the Immigration Department said: “Non-refoulement claimants are illegal immigrants, overstayers or persons who were refused entry upon arrival in Hong Kong.”
Regardless of the outcome of their claim, they cannot remain legally in the city, it said.
“For those claimants whose claims are substantiated, their removal will be withheld until their claimed risks cease to exist. Their immigration status would not change, regardless of the results of their claims, and they will not be issued with any form of travel documents,” the department said.

data.title: Has Hong Kong laid the groundwork to become a world-leading crypto hub?
data.description:
In late 2022, Syed Musheer Ahmed packed his bags and departed Hong Kong. The founder of fintech advisory firm FinStep Asia headed for Dubai, where he joined the founding team of the Gulf city’s new digital-asset regulator, Virtual Assets Regulatory Authority (VARA).
Like many in Hong Kong’s crypto scene at the time, Ahmed was drawn by the Middle East’s regulatory clarity – a stark contrast to the uncertainty and Covid-19 restrictions still lingering in his adopted home.
“Dubai came up as a good alternative” at that time, Ahmed recalled, noting that few places were offering crypto licensing for a large enough market then.
Now, Ahmed is back – and the Hong Kong he has returned to is very different from the one he left.


As Hong Kong’s Web3 regulations take shape and the city pushes to revive its digital-asset industry after a prolonged slump, Ahmed’s return reflects a broader shift. Industry insiders said Hong Kong now had a prime opportunity to leverage its unique status as a “superconnector” and provide the regulatory certainty that highly mobile crypto firms were searching for in a fragmented global landscape.
Hong Kong is expanding its digital-asset framework to encompass stablecoins, tokenisation and a broader Web3 infrastructure. As part of this push, the city is set to issue its first batch of stablecoin licences this month, with further legislation for digital-asset dealing and custodial services planned for the summer.
In the global race to attract Web3 firms, regulatory certainty had eclipsed financial perks as the ultimate differentiator between potential bases, according to industry insiders.
“Hong Kong is currently a top-tier contender in the global digital asset hierarchy,” said Alessio Quaglini, CEO and co-founder of digital-asset custodian Hex Trust. “We are seeing a shift where ‘regulation is the product’,” and “modern capital is seeking ‘regulatory clarity’ over ‘regulatory ease’.”




Cora Ang, head of legal and compliance for Asia-Pacific at Amina Bank, agreed. Capital was “flowing towards regulatory clarity, not regulatory leniency” and this clarity was driving institutional strategy, Ang said.
This demand for clarity has fundamentally changed how competing global Web3 hubs vie for business.
“Jurisdictions are competing not only on tax or incentives but also on regulatory clarity, quality of licensing and depth of market participation,” said Andrew Fei, a partner at law firm King & Wood Mallesons.
Hong Kong’s position as a “superconnector” between mainland China and global markets gave it a unique edge, Fei said.
That role is helping the city forge deeper ties with the Middle East.
“We see growing interest and expansion routes connecting Hong Kong and the Middle East,” said Sean McHugh, senior director and head of market assurance and licensing at VARA.
He said digital-asset firms were increasingly building dual-hub footprints, using Hong Kong to anchor Asia and the United Arab Emirates for Middle Eastern and global connectivity, citing the example of Hong Kong-listed exchange HashKey, which obtained a virtual asset service provider (VASP) licence from VARA in May.
Other Hong Kong firms with a Middle East presence include stablecoin issuer IDA, digital-asset company Animoca Brands and blockchain-based alternative asset investment platform Evident Capital.
Hong Kong’s advantages stand out in a fragmented landscape, given widely varying approaches to regulation and oversight around the world. The city’s digital-asset regulation falls under the purview of the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) and the Hong Kong Monetary Authority.
In the case of Dubai, VARA was set up to regulate digital assets.
Dubai stood out for its quick licensing turnaround times, according to Kristi Swartz, a partner at law firm DLA Piper. Depending on an application’s complexity, VARA’s approval process takes just three to six months, whereas a review by the SFC can stretch to anywhere from nine to 15 months.
“They [Dubai] are not just being purely crypto-friendly, they also know what they are doing, the risks involved and the benefits of implementing more innovations,” said Gracy Chen, CEO of crypto exchange Bitget.


Henri Arslanian, co-founder of Nine Blocks Capital Management, called the Middle East’s approach to crypto regulation “the most forward-looking globally by far”.
However, ongoing US and Israeli military strikes on Iran, coupled with Tehran’s threats against its neighbours, pose a severe risk to geopolitical stability, hindering the long-term prospects of the Middle East’s crypto industry.
While licensing speed and progressive regulations work in Dubai’s favour, the more fragmented regulatory model in the US creates a disadvantage.
There, digital-asset oversight was spread across multiple federal agencies, including the Securities and Exchange Commission and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, according to industry insiders. Individual US states also have their own licensing regimes, including BitLicense in New York and California’s Digital Financial Assets Law.
But Washington is moving to clear these hurdles. The Guiding and Establishing National Innovation for US Stablecoins Act, which came into effect in July last year, established the first federal regulatory framework for stablecoins in the US.
Once the US market structure became clearer, the potential pool of institutional demand would expand significantly, according to Edwin Mata, CEO of digital-asset firm Brickken.


Despite some shortcomings, the US stablecoin market still dwarfs the rest of the world. US dollar-pegged stablecoins accounted for more than 98 per cent of the US$306 billion global market as of late February, according to CoinGecko, the world’s largest independent cryptocurrency data aggregator.
The US also “remains the centre for institutional liquidity via exchange-traded funds”, according to Hex Trust’s Quaglini.
The euro-pegged stablecoin market is a distant second. With a market cap of US$911 million at the end of February, it represented less than 0.3 per cent of the overall stablecoin market, according to CoinGecko.
In Europe, the Markets in Crypto-Assets (Mica) regulation provides a unified rule book for all 27 EU member states, governing crypto issuance, services and market abuse.
According to Bitget’s Chen, MiCA was among the most comprehensive regulatory frameworks, which combined with Europe’s large user base made the region highly “appealing” for crypto exchanges.
That view is shared by Mata. Compared with Hong Kong, MiCA held a structural advantage in market breadth, scale and legal uniformity, but Europe trailed Hong Kong in legislative speed and agility, he said.


Mata added that firms issuing tokenised securities in Europe faced added complexity, as tokens qualifying as financial instruments fell under the Markets in Financial Instruments Directive II. The regulation, which came into effect in 2018, was developed to protect investors and addresses weaknesses in financial markets by moving more trading onto regulated and transparent platforms.
As European firms navigate these issues, global focus was shifting towards Asia, according to analysts and market data. The region has established itself as the world’s fastest-growing market for on-chain activity, with blockchain analytics firm Chainalysis reporting a 69 per cent year-on-year surge to US$2.36 trillion in transaction volume in the 12 months to June 2025.
Within this booming landscape, analysts said Hong Kong was emerging as a more agile player than regional rivals Japan and Singapore.
“Japan is one of the most prescriptive major jurisdictions,” said Mata, noting that while Japan’s strength was in robust investor protection and operational discipline, this came at a cost of higher compliance friction.
Hong Kong was much more “growth-oriented” by comparison, excelling at rapid regulatory iteration and institutional tokenisation structures, he said. Highlighting this divide, Mata said Japan treated stablecoins not as crypto assets, but as a distinct category integrated into its national payment infrastructure.
Meanwhile, Singapore’s regulatory approach was conservative, emphasising retail safeguards and payment stability through strict custody, leverage and advertising rules, while approving only a fraction of licence applicants, analysts said.
Hong Kong also offered clearer pathways for regulated retail participation, they added.


Beneath the surface, a new challenge is brewing for the emerging industry in Hong Kong. While Hong Kong rapidly built its digital-asset ecosystem, virtually every major jurisdiction was also trying to attract the same capital, talent and high‑quality firms, with the borderless nature of Web3 amplifying the fierce competition, King & Wood Mallesons’ Fei said.
“Most crypto firms don’t consider themselves bound by traditional geographic borders,” said Minh Do, chief operating officer of Animoca Brands. “They’ll go where there is business.”
Some industry observers raised concerns over the SFC’s rigorous legislative framework, saying the licensing regime required institutional-grade infrastructure, comprehensive risk management and significant capital commitments. Others said the regulatory environment was excessively stringent in some instances.
One example is the SFC’s mandatory “hot/cold storage” ratio. The SFC requires digital-asset firms to hold 98 per cent of assets in “cold wallets”, which are offline storage devices, and no more than 2 per cent in internet-connected “hot wallets”.
“That creates a very large burden for exchanges, and it certainly would for custodians,” said Robin Scott, general manager for Middle East and general counsel at AIFT, a cybersecurity insurance company, adding that the requirement was potentially counterproductive, as having almost all assets in a cold wallet necessitated frequent access, thereby compromising overall security.
The industry’s concerns are being recognised. In the “A-S-P-I-Re” document, a strategic road map set out by the SFC last year for regulating the digital-asset industry, the regulator admitted that such rigid storage ratios could lead to “operational impracticalities” for VASPs.
Scott said “stringent” seemed to be a common theme among regulators.
“I can’t think of any example where we would say, ‘Oh, that’s an easy licence to get’,” said Scott, adding that the 2022 collapse of the crypto exchange FTX, where billions in customer deposits and funds were embezzled by its owners, served as a wake-up call for regulators worldwide.
Industry participants are calling for the harmonisation of international regulatory standards as fragmentation compels global firms to create isolated operations, which runs counter to the borderless nature of blockchain technology.
Myles Harrison, chief product officer at Amina Bank, summed it up succinctly. The lack of regulatory harmonisation was “the biggest challenge that we as an industry need to overcome”, he said.

data.title: Xiaomi’s new-generation SU7 launch sparks fresh EV fight with Tesla in China
data.description:
Xiaomi’s updated SU7 electric vehicle (EV) is expected to bite into the market share of Tesla and some domestic rivals in China, as demand weakens and regulators tighten oversight.
Founder and CEO Lei Jun on Thursday announced official pricing for the new-generation smart sedan, with the standard edition starting at 219,900 yuan (US$31,900) and the pro and max variants set at 249,900 yuan and 303,900 yuan, respectively.
The figures are 10,000 yuan lower than pre-sale estimates, but 4,000 yuan higher than the first-generation SU7, which launched in March 2024 at 215,900 yuan.
Lei said the adjustment reflected significant improvements in vehicle configurations and rising supply chain costs, adding that the new model offered strong value for money.
Citibank described the pricing as fair and suggested the model’s mature monthly run-rate could return to 270,000 units, according to a note on Thursday.
“When compared with competing brands in the same price range, it is roughly on par or slightly better than peers, without very obvious advantages beyond the average level,” said Citi analyst Kyna Wong. “We believe it will marginally take some of the Tesla Model 3, Zeekr 007 and Xpeng P7 market share.”

Xiaomi’s first-generation SU7 delivered more than 246,400 units in 2025, outselling the Tesla Model 3 to become the bestselling premium pure electric sedan priced above 200,000 yuan in China. Total deliveries reached 381,000 units before sales ended in February.
Morgan Stanley, in another report, said the update was poised for success given high consumer interest and tangible feature upgrades, believing that the improvements in “smart cabin experience and safety measures” strengthened its competitive edge.
The launch came as China’s new-energy vehicle sales fell 7 per cent year on year to 1.7 million units in the first two months of 2026, following the halving of a 10 per cent sales tax exemption.
Regulators renewed their push for stricter oversight of the EV market and urged manufacturers to shift focus from price wars to technological innovation in domestic automotive chips and self-driving systems.
The new SU7 featured a triple-redundancy door handle design that complied with 2027 national safety standards, ensuring emergency access even after a collision, Xiaomi said. The series now standardised Xiaomi’s hyper-autonomous driving technology, powered by its cognitive large model, supporting garage-level navigation and complex scenario reasoning, it added. Other enhancements included improved battery range, motor power and cabin noise control and sun protection.
The launch event drew industry leaders including BYD chairman Wang Chuanfu, Xpeng CEO He Xiaopeng and Li Auto CEO Li Xiang.
Xiaomi also revealed its long-term ambitions, including plans to invest 60 billion yuan in artificial intelligence over the next three years to further integrate its ecosystem.
Despite excitement for the product, Xiaomi’s shares fell nearly 8 per cent in Hong Kong on Friday.

data.title: Hong Kong stocks mark third weekly loss as oil volatility pressures sentimentHang Seng Index falls for a third week as investors weigh the US-Israel war on Iran, oil swings and corporate earnings.
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks extended declines to cap a third consecutive weekly loss, as escalating Middle East tensions added volatility and Alibaba Group Holding reported a slump in earnings.
The Hang Seng Index fell 0.9 per cent to 25,277.32 at the close on Friday, down 0.7 per cent for the week. The Hang Seng Tech Index dropped 1.3 per cent.
On the mainland, the Shanghai Composite Index slid 1.2 per cent, closing below the 4,000-point mark for the first time this year, while the CSI 300 Index retreated 0.4 per cent.
Alibaba Group Holding tumbled 6.3 per cent to HK$123.70 after reporting a 67 per cent slump in quarterly earnings and sluggish revenue growth. Xiaomi sank 8.6 per cent to HK$33.20 after launching a new version of its electric vehicle.
Crude oil remained in focus as investors watched fuel prices for clues on whether the US-Israel war on Iran would thrust the world into stagflation or even a recession. Futures contracts on the fuel eased more than 2 per cent on Friday after Israel said it would no longer target energy infrastructure and Iran’s military capability had been degraded. Still, Brent oil traded above US$100 a barrel, a level unsettling investors over higher energy costs.
“The stagflation trade is weighing on the markets,” said Cheng Qiang, an analyst at Topsperity Securities. “The correction may extend and investors should keep a close eye on developments in the Middle East and domestic policies.”
Stocks linked to space-based solar power jumped on the mainland after chipmaker Nvidia launched a platform for space computing, which investors believed paved the way for further expansion of the industry. Wuxi Autowell Technology jumped 3.8 per cent to 81.39 yuan in Shanghai, Dalian Linton NC Machine rallied 6.1 per cent to 40.40 yuan in Beijing and Suzhou Maxwell Technologies gained 9.2 per cent to 273.60 yuan in Shenzhen.
By 2030, the market for space-based solar power may be three times the size of ground-based solar power in 2025, according to JPMorgan Chase.
Elsewhere, Chinese printed circuit board maker Delton Technology gained 34 per cent to HK$96 on the first day of trading in Hong Kong.
Chinese stocks have emerged as outperformers in the latest oil shock, reversing their reputation as laggards, as crude takes a back seat to renewable fuels in the world’s second-largest economy.
The CSI 300 Index of stocks trading on the mainland’s exchanges has dropped 3.1 per cent since the US and Israel began attacks on Iran on February 28, outperforming the S&P 500, the Euro Stoxx 50 and Japan’s Nikkei 225, which have all slid at least 4 per cent during the period.
Other major Asian markets were mixed. South Korea’s Kospi rose 0.3 per cent, while Australia’s S&P/ASX 200 fell 0.8 per cent and Taiwan’s Taiex lost 0.4 per cent. Japan’s market was closed for a holiday.

data.title: Police officers fire 5 shots at charging assailant carrying serrated knife, metal rod
data.description:
Three Hong Kong police officers fired five shots at a man, suspected to be mentally ill, who charged at them with a serrated knife and a metal rod in the early hours of Saturday morning.
Senior Superintendent Iu Wing-kan said that police received a report at 12.17am of a man behaving abnormally and carrying weapons as he wandered around Tsuen Wan MTR station.
Another report came in at 12.44am of the armed man on Cheung Wing Road in Kwai Chung.
Officers were dispatched to both locations, but found no sign of the suspect.
The force was alerted by several drivers at around 1am that the man was on the Kwai Chung section of the Castle Peak Road and brandishing a serrated knife and a metal rod.
When members of police’s emergency unit arrived at the scene to search for him, the man suddenly jumped out of the bushes and charged at the officers.
Officers issued verbal warnings before using pepper spray to try to stop him, which proved ineffective.
Three officers then fired five shots at the man to subdue him.

Providing a detailed account of the decision to open fire, Iu said: “The man continued charging at our colleagues with his weapons, yelling ‘I’ll kill you’.”
“After warnings were ignored, one of our officers fired two shots at the man in an attempt to stop him.”
“After the shooting, the man did not stop his attack and continued to chase our colleagues, intending to harm them with his weapons.
“At this point, another officer fired a shot in hopes of subduing him, but it was ineffective. Finally, a third officer fired two shots at him, finally subduing him,” he said.
The shots were fired from distances of two to five metres (6.6 to 16.4 feet) and the scene was brightly lit, he said.
The senior superintendent said the incident took place in the space of several minutes, and defended the officers’ decision to open fire.
“Opening fire at a suspect is not an easy decision. I believe my colleagues made this difficult decision only after giving ample warnings, when they had no other choice, and ultimately acted decisively to subdue someone who could have posed a danger to police officers and even to the general public,” he said.
“When the last two shots were fired, and we were able to subdue the suspect, we immediately ceased all force.”
The man was sent to Princess Margaret Hospital in Kwai Chung, and was undergoing surgery. Two bullet holes were found in his right chest and right thigh.
Iu said the man, a 43-year-old Hong Kong identity card holder, was a construction worker and had no previous criminal record.
A backpack, believed to belong to the man, was found at the scene and contained camping sleeping bags, thermal blankets, clothes, cash and his identity documents.
Iu said the reports had described the man as wearing a dress and muttering to himself.
He also said that the man was suspected of having mental problems, adding that police would investigate the case, including possible motives.

data.title: Huawei challenges Nvidia with powerful new AI accelerator card
data.description:
Chinese tech giant Huawei Technologies has launched the Atlas 350 accelerator card for inference, boasting higher computing power for artificial intelligence applications and better performance than US rival Nvidia’s H20, as AI rapidly advances into the agentic era.
The Atlas 350 AI accelerator card would be powered by Huawei’s latest Ascend 950PR chip, designed to deliver enhanced computing power and storage for AI inferencing, said Ma Haixu, a vice-president at Huawei, at the firm’s China Partner Conference on Friday.
The card delivered 1.56 petaflops of FP4 computing power, a 2.8-times improvement over Nvidia’s China-tailored H20 chip, according to Zhang Dixuan, head of Huawei’s Ascend computing business. FP4 refers to low precision in computing, which allows the Atlas 350 accelerator to move data faster.
The accelerator card aimed to match or even exceed its industry peers in AI inference for search recommendation, multimodal generation and large language models, Zhang said.
An accelerator card is a hardware unit dedicated to a specialised task and designed to be integrated into a server.
The launch came as the US-sanctioned company ramped up efforts in AI computing infrastructure powered by its self-developed semiconductors, after achieving chip breakthroughs in recent years, including its Ascend AI chips, without relying on American technology.
In September, Huawei unveiled the Ascend 950PR, designed for prefill and recommendation, as part of the firm’s three-year road map for its Ascend ambition. Prefill is a fundamental step in AI model inference that ensures all input tokens are efficiently processed.
Huawei is also accelerating its computing hardware strategy amid the rapid rise of agentic AI, which has drastically increased demand for computing power and data to enable agentic AI systems to make plans and execute.
The Shenzhen-based company said it planned to make sweeping upgrades to its storage products this year, including its all-flash systems OceanStor Dorado and Pacific 9926 for enterprise-level storage. Huawei will also launch the FusionCube A1000 cabinet that enables quick AI deployment for small and medium-sized enterprises.
“While the first half of the AI era focused on computing power, the second half will be defined by data,” said Yuan Yuan, president of Huawei data storage product line. “In 2026, Huawei will continue to upgrade its storage product lines, and be closely involved with major national data infrastructure projects.”

data.title: Should the Hong Kong stock exchange make all IPO applications confidential?
data.description:
Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing (HKEX) last week proposed its biggest set of listing reforms since 2018 for Asia’s third-largest stock exchange. Delivered in a consultation paper, the reforms aim to further improve the competitiveness of the exchange that topped the world rankings for initial public offerings (IPOs) last year.
The bourse operator will collect views on the proposals, which include broadening a special listing regime for innovative companies and opening the door for listings by smaller and more diverse firms, until May 8.
Investment banks and brokers have piped up in support of a proposal to allow all companies that are pursuing listings to file their applications confidentially – a privilege currently given only to specific types of companies. Market participants believe the reform would attract more listings and enhance Hong Kong as an international financial centre because many other exchanges around the world already keep applications confidential.
Here is what you need to know about how the change would affect the market.
What is the current situation?
Since October 2013, HKEX has required all listing candidates to publish their listing documents, including corporate history, business models and financial information, on the HKEX website once they submit the application to the exchange.
The requirement aims to enhance market transparency by providing more information for investors and research analysts at the early stage of the listing process.
It also aims to improve the quality of listing documents, as listing sponsors are expected to step up their efforts to ensure public documents are up to standard.
The current rules allow confidential filing only for overseas-listed companies seeking a secondary listing, certain biotechnological firms and other technology applicants.
Those listing candidates can – and all candidates under the proposal could – give listing documents to the exchange for private vetting upon application submission, only publishing the information online after listing approval.
Why consider a change?
“The key benefit is the removal of a significant deterrent for high-quality companies,” said Rocky Tung Yat-ngok, executive director of the Financial Services Development Council, a government think tank.
“Currently, mandatory immediate disclosure exposes commercially sensitive information, including business strategies, to competitors during the regulatory process. Confidential filing protects companies during this sensitive phase, making Hong Kong a more attractive listing destination for potential issuers.”

HKEX head of listing Katherine Ng said the proposal for confidential filing by all candidates would ensure equal treatment for companies and bring Hong Kong in line with practices in other major markets.
What other markets allow confidential filing?
The stock markets in the UK and Singapore have no publication requirements for draft prospectuses upon submission of listing applications. The US had expanded confidential filing to all candidates in recent years, according to the HKEX consultation paper.
“Many overseas major markets, such as the stock exchanges in the US and UK, allow listing candidates to have confidential filing,” said John Lee Chen-kwok, vice-chairman and co-head of Asia coverage at UBS in Hong Kong.
“This will enhance the appeal and competitiveness of the Hong Kong stock markets to listing candidates, particularly those in innovative industries, as early disclosure of their technology and business operation details may affect their competitiveness and business operations.”
Hong Kong lawmaker Robert Lee Wai-wang, chairman of Grand Finance Group, said he supported the rule change, noting that the gap between filing an application and receiving approval may be several months.
“The current rule requires companies to disclose a lot of sensitive information for several months before they can get approval for listing,” he said. “In some cases, some companies may have disclosed all this information, but eventually they cannot get the approval.”
That could discourage good candidates from listing in Hong Kong, leading them to opt for the US or London, he added.
“It is fair to the companies to make the disclosure only after they have obtained the approval for listing.”
What about transparency concerns?
Ng said the change would not exempt companies from providing information to investors; it would just change the timing.
“The exchange would still allow companies to opt for publication of their listing documents once they apply for listing,” she added.

Will the change affect the quality of listing documents?
In December, market regulator the Securities and Futures Commission and HKEX ordered 13 investment banks to review their procedures and staffing after finding widespread deficiencies in IPO submissions. The regulator said on Thursday that it would soon begin inspections of investment banks to make sure they were meeting expectations.
HKEX’s listing reform proposal includes an enhanced return mechanism as a powerful safeguard to ensure accountability and deter poor-quality filings.
Under the proposal, HKEX would return substandard documents and identify all responsible professional parties – sponsors, lawyers and accountants – to the public.
“We believe it strikes the right balance of protecting commercial confidentiality early on to attract more issuers, while ensuring the public receives full, vetted information before any investment decision,” the HKEX paper said.

data.title: Hong Kong’s ‘ghost oil’ stations rake in ‘HK$10,000 a day’ as petrol prices soar
data.description:
Operators of illicit fuel stations can earn as much as HK$10,000 (US$1,280) a day by smuggling untaxed petrol into Hong Kong using vehicles equipped with enlarged tanks and selling it to car club members via coded messages, the South China Morning Post has learned.
A source said there had been more cases involving illicit fuel or “ghost oil” in the city since late February, when petrol prices began to soar following US-Israel strikes on Iran.
The source said law enforcement raids usually discovered trucks equipped with enlarged tanks filled with untaxed fuel from mainland China, vehicles acting as mobile stations carrying untaxed petrol, and fixed illicit filling stations.
Fixed stations were usually hidden in sheds in remote areas, the source said. Operators would offload the illicit fuel from their vehicles into barrels at the site for patrons or fill up other vehicles acting as mobile stations elsewhere.
“There are transit points for the fuel to be distributed to other areas,” the source said.
Illicit filling stations could earn up to HK$10,000 per day, according to the source, as they profited from price differences between local taxed petrol and lower-priced options on the mainland.
“Take 95-octane petrol as an example, it costs around 8 yuan [US$1.16] on the mainland, which is around HK$9. In Hong Kong, petrol costs around HK$30 a litre, maybe around HK$25 to HK$26 with a discount included,” the source said.
Hong Kong does not sell 95-octane petrol, a cheaper variety that is widely available on the mainland and in other countries. Local fuel stations offer 98-octane unleaded petrol instead, which is more expensive.

Consumer Council data showed that Friday’s pump price for standard petrol stood at HK$31.09 a litre, while the net price after a walk-in discount ranged between HK$23.09 and HK$30.19.
The source said illicit vendors would earn a tidy profit even by selling their fuel HK$2 or HK$3 above the mainland price.
“For a station that stocks 1,000 to 2,000 litres, the profit will be in the thousands, with revenue up to HK$10,000 a day,” the source said.
Toyota Estima owner Jack Chan*, 39, has been using illegal fuel for seven years.
“Many drivers join car clubs, and these clubs have WhatsApp groups. Usually, the sources for ‘ghost oil’ are found within these groups,” Chan, an engineer, said. “We don’t explicitly say we want ‘ghost oil’; we use the code ‘I want to drink milk’ instead.”
Chan said fuel providers would message him upon seeing the code words to exchange contact details. The supplier and Chan would then meet at a remote location, usually in Yuen Long, where Chan lives.


Filling the 60-litre tank in his car with illicit petrol, Chan can save up to HK$600. He pays about HK$800 to HK$900 to fill his tank with “ghost oil”, while the legal option would cost between HK$1,300 and HK$1,500.
Chan said suppliers drove modified seven-seaters, with the rear seats removed to make room for a large fuel tank.
“They come equipped with their own fuel nozzles to pump the petrol into my car,” he said. “The nozzle has an electronic meter, so it’s all digitised. I can clearly see how much fuel I have taken, and then we settle the transaction in cash.”
Authorities have also focused on clamping down on illegal fuel stations amid the price surge. A joint crackdown by the Customs and Excise Department and the Fire Services Department on three illegal fuelling stations this week resulted in two people being arrested and the seizure of more than 1,800 litres of petrol estimated to be worth HK$54,000.
A spokeswoman for customs said 18 illicit fuel operations had been smashed as of February. Officers seized 19,974 litres of illegal fuel and 12 vehicles, and arrested 17 people.
