“Customs will continue to work closely with relevant departments, conduct joint operations when appropriate to holistically tackle illegal fuelling activities. We will also apply to the court to confiscate the vehicles involved,” the spokeswoman said.

The Fire Services Department said that it received 221 complaints about illegal petrol stations in the first two months of this year, or about 111 per month – 42 per cent higher than the monthly average of 78 cases in 2025.
The department seized 12,763 litres of petrol and 180,454 litres of diesel this year as of February 28, instituting 76 prosecutions.
Ringo Lee Yiu-pui, governor and honorary life president of the Hong Kong, China Automobile Association, said substandard fuel used to be sold illegally at lower prices.
However, the supply of this type of poor-quality illegal fuel was reduced after the government removed duties for Euro V diesel in 2008.
“Illicit filling stations could simply use the proper fuel [bought elsewhere]. That was in a sense legal as there were no duties, but illegal stations were still illegal for violating fire safety laws,” Lee said.
However, petrol is taxed in the city, rendering sales beyond established filling stations illegal.
Lee said while most illegal filling stations supplied diesel, a cheaper fuel widely used by commercial vehicles, he had noticed an uptick in those providing petrol recently.
“If the illicit station provides petrol, it’s more dangerous as the fuel is more volatile than diesel,” Lee warned.
Lee urged the government to introduce balance mechanisms to regulate fuel prices to reduce demand for cheaper illegal alternatives.
*The interviewee’s name has been changed.

data.title: Hong Kong police hunt 3 suspects over US$12 million gold theft
data.description:
Hong Kong police have launched a manhunt for three people involved in a suspected deception case in which a shop reportedly lost 73kg of gold bars worth an estimated US$12 million, the South China Morning Post has learned.
The force said on Friday that a woman in Hung Hom had made a report around 2.57pm that a nearby shop was suspected to have been “burgled”. The woman said the shop owner had told her someone had stolen the gold bars before fleeing in a private vehicle.
A source said the case involved a privately owned gold shop located in the Peninsula Square commercial building on Sung On Street.
A former partner of the shop allegedly tricked the manager into handing over gold bars worth around US$12 million, the source said. The man then got into a black Tesla with his wife and a male driver and fled the scene.
The force said that during the investigation officers pulled over a white vehicle, but it was later determined to be unrelated to the case.
The car was reportedly stopped near a toll plaza on the North Lantau Highway on the way to the airport.
The source said the case had been classified as “obtaining property by deception”.

data.title: Residents of Tai Po blaze building misled over renovation project, inquiry told
data.description:
Residents of a housing complex destroyed in one of Hong Kong’s deadliest fires were misled into picking a convicted contractor for the estate’s renovation project amid “widespread” bid-rigging and other corruption in the construction industry, a public hearing has been told.
The judge-led independent committee also heard on Friday that an engineering consultant allegedly accepted a monthly stipend to “rubber stamp” tender evaluation reports, including one recommending Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po pick the most expensive option offered by a contractor .
In his opening speech on the second day of a series of evidential hearings, the committee’s leading senior counsel, Victor Dawes, highlighted what appeared to be suspicious connections between the contractor and five of its competitors in the tendering process, suggesting they could have “some forms of relationship” under the table.
Dawes said various law enforcement agencies shared the view that bid-rigging and other anticompetitive practices were rife in the city, with police noting that people with triad backgrounds had set up engineering and construction firms hoping to monopolise the building renovation business.
“The information received from law enforcement is consistent, that such practice is widespread and common in the market,” he said.

The inferno that tore through seven of Wang Fuk Court’s eight blocks in November last year was the deadliest in Hong Kong since 1948, killing 168 people and leaving nearly 5,000 homeless.
All eight buildings had been under renovation and covered with bamboo scaffolding and mesh netting since the summer of 2024.
The HK$336 million (US$42.89 million) renovation contract for Prestige Construction and Engineering Company, the priciest among 57 proposals offered, was passed under unusual circumstances earlier that year, as 570 votes were reportedly cast despite only 293 residents, including those represented by authorised people, being present.
The then chairman of the estate’s management committee later ignored residents’ requests to convene a meeting to overturn the decision.
In 2021, residents chose Will Power Architects Company as the project’s consultant after it offered to conduct an initial inspection of the estate and to oversee the work for HK$220,000 and HK$308,000 respectively, well below the market rate
In an evaluation report in 2024, Will Power picked Prestige from 57 contractors as its “first choice”, highlighting its “clear” criminal record over the preceding eight years.
Dawes said the consultant’s director, Wilson Ng Yeuk, who claimed to have approved the report, had likely not reviewed the bids at all.

The barrister said Prestige was convicted 24 times for occupational safety breaches between 2017 and 2023.
After a Will Power employee noticed the report had been amended to give Prestige a high rating for its “clear” record – a change that ultimately placed the contractor above the rest in the final ranking – another company director, Wong Hap-yin, told him to continue using the modified version.
“We believe residents had already been misled at this stage,” Dawes said, adding that Wong could have deliberately falsified the document.
The evidence also suggested that Ng, who was employed by Swire Properties at the time of the fire, had admitted to working as a “freelancer” for Will Power for a monthly stipend of HK$15,000 to act as a registered inspector, earning a total of HK$190,000.
“He could very likely have simply been a rubber stamp, putting his signature to whatever document was given to him,” Dawes added.




Ng sold all his shares at Will Power to Wong for HK$1 on the evening of November 26, hours after the fire began.
The apparent interconnections between Prestige and five other competitors in the bidding process have also come under scrutiny.
Prestige’s director, Hau Wa-kin, jointly owned a company called Rich Surplus Develop with Richie Tam Chee-man, who was the director of competitor Man Sing General Contractors.
Leung Ping-yiu, the director of Widely Construction & Engineering, was formerly a director of Prestige.
Hau also owned a now-dissolved company, Blazing Edge, together with Li Chik-hoi and Choi Man-ming, who were directors of San Hoi Ming Construction Engineering and Sunarts (Holdings) Engineering respectively. Li’s and Choi’s companies had also bid for the Wang Fuk Court project.
Blazing Edge had shared the same company secretary as Mega Praise Construction & Engineering, another bidder.
Dawes said Hau, who declined to give live evidence at the hearings, had failed to give satisfactory answers to the committee’s queries.
The rapid spread of the fire and the high death toll could be partly due to the use of polyfoam boards to cover windows, replacement of fireproof windows with wooden doors in evacuation passages and the failure of fire safety systems, the committee heard.
Dawes said the use of combustible boards had triggered repeated complaints from residents, but the Fire Services Department and the Housing Bureau’s Independent Checking Unit both believed the concerns fell outside their area of supervision.
Prestige purchased a new set of polyfoam boards in 2024 to address the complaints.
Text messages in October 2024 revealed that Tony Tsui moon-come, then chairman of the estate’s management committee, told Prestige director Gordon Ho Kin-yip to find a sample that was “not so flammable” to conduct a fire test and show the result to residents, adding a video to be taken should not be “too fake”.
Ho replied the first batch of boards would not be replaced.
Dawes said the exchange, while incomplete, at least showed the pair’s understanding that the contractor might have purchased substandard items and existing flammable boards would not be removed.
The barrister also took the Independent Checking Unit to task for failing to make an on-site inspection of the illegal alterations to Wang Fuk Court’s evacuation staircases.
Dawes pointed out that replacing some windows with wooden doors had allowed toxic gas from outside to spread into homes during the fire.
He also said the estate’s fire safety contractor, China Status Development and Engineering, had failed to assess the necessity of shutting down the fire hosepipes and alarms for more than half a year, leading to the almost complete failure of fire safety measures when the blaze broke out.
Tsui also gave a statement on Friday, saying he and other management committee members had been subject to unfair criticisms after the fire and he was confident the committee could “restore the truth”.
“I appreciate the [independent] committee’s effort to compile such a large sum of information and restore the truth,” he said tearfully.
“I know that there are many misunderstandings about us from the public, and I am grateful to the committee for allowing us to speak out about what we know.”

Outside the hearing, Secretary for Labour and Welfare Chris Sun Yuk-han said the Labour Department would fully cooperate with the probe, after the first session on Thursday revealed that no action had been taken against smoking workers after 16 site checks despite residents’ repeated complaints.
“If there is a need for accountability, it will be pursued to the very end,” Sun said.
Legal counsel for the government will deliver an opening statement when the hearings resume at City Gallery in Central on Tuesday. The first witness may testify that afternoon.

data.title: Swire Coca-Cola wins HK$32 million government deal after bottled water scandal
data.description:
Swire Coca-Cola has secured a HK$31.9 million (US$4 million) contract to supply Bonaqua water to some Hong Kong government offices, following a HK$52.9 million bottled water procurement scandal last year.
According to a Government Logistics Department notice, Swire Coca-Cola was awarded the 27-month contract on Tuesday to supply more than 1.46 million bottles of drinking water to offices on Hong Kong Island and parts of the outlying islands. The selection followed an open tender launched in December.
In its tender documents, the department added several requirements, including requiring suppliers to prove they held a market share over the past 10 years, had a minimum of 50 sales channels, and possessed a daily order delivery capacity of at least 100 orders.
If the bottled water is imported, the supplier must meet the qualifications of a food importer and submit a safety test report. If the total bid price exceeds HK$15 million, bidders must pass a financial review, pay a contract deposit and allow authorities to conduct an on-site inspection.
In June 2023, Swire Coca-Cola also won a bid to supply bottled water involving 1.35 million bottles of drinking water for HK$40.5 million.
The government came under scrutiny last August over a three-year HK$52.9 million bottled water supply contract awarded to Xin Ding Xin Trade. It was the first time mainland Chinese suppliers won the contract, supposedly saving the city’s 170,000 civil servants an average of about HK$16.2 million a year on bottled water supplies.
The company allegedly submitted fraudulent documents and is suspected of defrauding the department and breaching the Trade Descriptions Ordinance.
Following the scandal, authorities promptly terminated the contract and said they would pick a new supplier of bottled drinking water for government offices on Hong Kong Island and some outlying islands through open tendering.

Before the new contract took effect, the department arranged for Watson Group to temporarily supply bottled drinking water to government offices on Hong Kong Island and some outlying islands for a period of four months, amounting to about HK$7.1 million.
The Financial Services and the Treasury Bureau said the government’s procurement process would involve strengthened requirements, with minister Christopher Hui Ching-yu chairing a task force set up in the wake of the scandal in August, to review the procurement process.
They include stipulating specific due diligence requirements, expanding the roles of the logistics department’s audit team and revising standard government contracts to empower authorities to conduct site inspections during the vetting process.
After the scandal emerged, a separate government audit review released in October last year revealed that the logistics department had repeatedly ignored signs of fraud – including an incorrect name on the company chop – during the vetting process and in the initial stage of contract operations.
This oversight allowed the company, accused of allegedly using falsified water quality test certificates, to win the government tender and supply substandard products.
A police investigation found that Xin Ding Xin Trade sourced water from a Dongguan plant instead of the Guangzhou supplier named in its contract.
The force also discovered the company had no connection with the Guangzhou company it claimed to be sourcing water from.
Instead, the firm used a water quality certificate provided by the Guangzhou plant to meet tender requirements, without the plant’s knowledge.
The scandal last year also prompted the government to rescind the head of the logistics department Carlson Chan Ka-shun’s Silver Bauhinia Star nomination.
